__TR_logo-retention-churn_0__: SaaS Logo Retention vs. Revenue Churn: What Buyers Miss | Churn Lens
__TR_logo-retention-churn_1__: The difference between logo retention and revenue churn — and why focusing on the wrong metric costs buyers millions. Free analysis from subscription CSVs.
__TR_logo-retention-churn_2__: SaaS Logo Retention vs. Revenue Churn: What Buyers Miss
__TR_logo-retention-churn_3__: Why logo retention and revenue churn tell different stories — and how to use both in buyer-side SaaS due diligence.
__TR_logo-retention-churn_4__: SaaS Logo Retention vs. Revenue Churn
__TR_logo-retention-churn_5__: Why logo retention and revenue churn tell different stories.
__TR_logo-retention-churn_16__: Two metrics that should agree — but when they diverge, the gap reveals exactly where the risk is hiding in a SaaS acquisition target.
__TR_logo-retention-churn_17__: A seller shows you 90% logo retention. Sounds great. But does that 90% represent customers paying $10/mo or $1,000/mo? If the 10% of lost logos were your highest-value accounts, the 90% retention number is actively misleading. This is the logo vs. revenue churn divergence — and it's one of the most common traps in buyer-side SaaS due diligence.
__TR_logo-retention-churn_24__: % of customers retained over a period
__TR_logo-retention-churn_26__: Losing high-value logos looks same as losing $5/mo users
__TR_logo-retention-churn_28__: % of MRR lost to cancellations + downgrades
__TR_logo-retention-churn_29__: How the revenue base is trending
__TR_logo-retention-churn_30__: Downgrades are invisible; expansion masks decay
__TR_logo-retention-churn_33__: Whether the revenue base grows organically
__TR_logo-retention-churn_34__: Expansion can hide massive contraction — always check both
__TR_logo-retention-churn_35__: Three Divergence Patterns to Watch For
__TR_logo-retention-churn_36__: Pattern 1: High Logo Retention, High Revenue Churn
__TR_logo-retention-churn_37__: This means you're keeping most customers but losing revenue from the ones you keep. Caused by:
__TR_logo-retention-churn_38__: Downgrades — customers moving to cheaper plans
__TR_logo-retention-churn_39__: Seat reduction — team/org accounts shrinking headcount
__TR_logo-retention-churn_40__: Usage-based pricing contraction — customers using less
__TR_logo-retention-churn_41__: This is the most insidious pattern because the headline retention number looks great. Churn Lens flags it by computing revenue churn side-by-side with logo retention and highlighting any gap wider than 5 percentage points.
__TR_logo-retention-churn_42__: Pattern 2: Low Logo Retention, Low Revenue Churn
__TR_logo-retention-churn_43__: You're losing many customers but the revenue impact is small. This usually means the churn is concentrated in low-value accounts — a problematic signal in itself if those low-value accounts are supposed to upgrade over time (the "land and expand" strategy isn't working).
__TR_logo-retention-churn_44__: Pattern 3: Logo Retention Improving, Revenue Churn Worsening
__TR_logo-retention-churn_45__: The most dangerous trending pattern. The company has improved product stickiness (fewer people leave) but existing customers are paying less. This often precedes a structural price cut or feature commoditization — and it won't show in a standard CIM.
